EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southeast Louisiana during the morning hours of the 22nd. The thunderstorms associated with the cold front produced at least one tornado near Galliano.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-1 tornado touched down near the intersection of Old Safari Heights and Louisiana Highway 3235, where it tore the roof off of a business housed in a double-wide manufactured home. It also tore fascia off of a drive-in restaurant. It turned 2 vehicles in the parking lot and tossed one small car approximately 15 feet into a field. From there it moved east-northeast toward Main Street. As it approached Main Street, it broke several large limbs from hardwood trees, and tore the car port off of a home. Metal roof covering was also peeled back from a home. As the tornado moved across Bayou Lafourche, it turned more toward the northeast and caused shingle damage to one home. It then turned more toward the southeast, and caused damage to trees and a few power poles. The storm lifted as it neared the South Lafourche High School Football Stadium. Maximum winds were estimated at 100 mph, with a path length near 1 mile and a path width of 30 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough of low pressure progressed east across the Southern Plains during the afternoon of April 13th, with a large warm sector of warm/humid air having expanded north across much of the Ark-La-Tex. A capping inversion delayed the onset of thunderstorm development until the late afternoon, where it eroded and large scale forcing increased with shortwave perturbations ahead of the trough and along/just ahead of a dry line as it mixed east to the Interstate 35 corridor or North and Central Texas. Given the moderate instability and strong wind shear that developed with the lead shortwave, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across Northcentral Louisiana during the late afternoon through mid evening hours, producing numerous reports of damaging winds, and several tornadoes/funnel cloud reports. While a lull in the storms was observed, a line of severe thunderstorms developed along the dryline south of the Dallas/Fort Worth Metroplex, and progressed east into East Texas by late evening, and across North Louisiana after midnight on April 14th. Damaging winds and reports of hail were prevalent at first with this line of severe thunderstorms, but a number of notches developed along the squall line as these storms crossed into North Louisiana, resulting in several tornadoes touching down across areas along and north of the Interstate 20 corridor from Shreveport to Monroe. Of particular interest, the initial string of tornadoes first touched down just west of the Texas/Louisiana state line along the Harrison/Panola County line, with this supercell producing two separate tornadoes as it entered Shreveport. In fact, the tornado remained on the ground across much of western and central Shreveport, moving across the northern fringes of the property at the National Weather Service Office before moving across the Queensborough and Allendale neighborhoods and across Downtown Shreveport, before tracking across much of Bossier City along and just north of Interstate 20, crossing Interstate 220, and across the Red Chute and Princeton communities before lifting just west of the Bossier/Webster Parish line. These severe thunderstorms exited North Louisiana just after 3 am on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very brief EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds of 85-95 mph touched down along Laster Road where it snapped a few trees. The tornado then crossed Highway 143 and continued northeast, snapping and uprooting more trees before lifting along Highway 2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds near 105 mph touched down just west of Highway 3132 and Interstate 20 on Dixie Boulevard, where it uprooted a few trees. The tornado continued east northeast crossing Highway 3132 snapping several large branches from trees along and just north of the National Weather Service Shreveport office but across the northern fringes of the Shreveport Regional Airport, before crossing Hollywood Avenue and tearing off portions of the roofs of three hotels on Monkhouse Drive near Interstate 20. Portions of the roof off of the Shreveport Fire Department Station #16 near the entrance of the Shreveport Regional Airport was also ripped off, and several nearby trees were uprooted as well. The tornado paralleled Interstate 20 where it uprooted trees and damaged a few billboard signs. The tornado then shifted north northeast crossing Jewella Avenue near Jackson Street where it began to cause significant tree damage in this area. The tornado swelled to around 800 yards wide where it uprooted and snapped numerous trees along a 13 block path east northeast to the vicinity of the intersection of Hearne Avenue and Lakeshore Drive in the Queensborough neighborhood. Major structural damage occurred to a two-story home at the 2600 block of Stonewall Street when a large tree was uprooted and fell onto the home in Queensborough. Additional major structural damage occurred to the Galilee Learning Center at the 1600 block of Gary Street in Queensborough as well. The tornado continued northeast into Downtown Shreveport causing sporadic tree damage before crossing the Red River into Downtown Bossier City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down near the Plantation Hotel, uplifting a large section of the roof and breaking multiple room windows. The tornado continued southeast across US 90 into the marsh and dissipated. The max estimated wind speed was 80 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds near 100 mph touched down along Highway 2 just west of Dogwood Road where it uprooted several trees and snapped several large branches. The tornado continued northeast where it uprooted and snapped a number of trees along White Oak Road near Kelly Road, eventually crossing the intersection of Ruple Road and Slaughter Road where it rapidly intensified. The tornado did considerable tree damage as it paralleled Slaughter Road and crossed the Louisiana and Northwest railroad track and Bayou D'Arbonne. It uprooted and snapped numerous trees in this area as it tracked northeast to Highway 520. At the intersection of Slaughter Road and Highway 520, it continued to uproot and snap trees as the tornado began to parallel Highway 520. The tornado began to weaken as it moved northeast along Highway 520, causing sporadic tree damage before lifting near Moore Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado was estimated maximum winds near 115 mph touched down about 4 miles east of Bryceland and moved east along Jot 'Em Down Road, snapping and uprooting several trees. Two barns were completely destroyed, the tops of several hardwood trees were snapped, and three-quarters of a metal roof of a home was uplifted and blown into the trees, but all exterior walls were still left standing. The tornado continued moving east, snapping and uprooting trees before lifting along Mount Calm Road, just south of the Bienville/Lincoln Parish line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the continuation of the Northeast Bossier Parish tornado. This EF-0 tornado, with estimated maximum winds near 80 mph, continued northeast traveling across Highway 2 in Sarepta uprooting a few trees, before eventually crossing Highway 371 just north of Sarepta where most of the damage occurred. Several trees were uprooted and snapped in this area especially along North Main Street, with a tree falling on a home, before the tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of Friday, April 13th, scattered supercells developed across northeast Louisiana within the broad warm sector of a strong surface cyclone centered near the Kansas/Nebraska border. These storms exhibited rotation almost immediately, and began producing tornadoes across northern Louisiana. Two tornadoes occurred in the NWS Jackson forecast area in association with a cell that tracked from near Rayville, LA north northeastward along the Boeuf River. These storms also produced strong wind as they continued into southeast Arkansas. Overnight, as the cold front associated with the storm system began to advance eastward, a quasi-linear convective system developed over the ArkLaTex and advanced eastward. These storms reached the western portion of the NWS Jackson forecast area after 2:30 am on Saturday, April 14th. Six tornadoes occurred during the early morning hours across southeast Arkansas and northeast Louisiana in association with this line of storms. The storms continued to produce damaging straight-line winds, knocking down trees and power lines in many areas, through the morning hours as it continued eastward into Mississippi. A few additional tornadoes also developed along the line, including one in Flowood, one near Noxapater, one in western Lamar County, and an EF2 tornado in Meridian.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were flooded north of Pioneer in the western portion of West Carroll Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across much of East Texas and Southwest Arkansas by the afternoon hours on April 6th, with very warm, humid, and unstable air having spread in place across much of the Ark-La-Tex during the afternoon and evening hours. In addition to the surface based instability in place, very cold temperatures in the mid and upper level of the atmosphere resulted in very steep lapse rates further enhancing instability, with strong wind shear present in advance of a strong cold front which entered the Southern Plains during the afternoon ahead of a strong upper level trough of low pressure. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along and just in advance of the cold front, many of which became severe producing large hail and damaging winds. These storms steadily marched east across North Louisiana during the mid afternoon through much of the evening hours before diminishing late with the passage of the cold front and upper trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell in the Marion community.

Of particular interest, the initial string of tornadoes first touched down just west of the Texas/Louisiana state line along the Harrison/Panola County line, with this supercell producing two separate tornadoes as it entered Shreveport. In fact, the tornado remained on the ground across much of western and central Shreveport, moving across the northern fringes of the property at the National Weather Service Office before moving across the Queensborough and Allendale neighborhoods and across Downtown Shreveport, before tracking across much of Bossier City along and just north of Interstate 20, crossing Interstate 220, and across the Red Chute and Princeton communities before lifting just west of the Bossier/Webster Parish line. These severe thunderstorms exited North Louisiana just after 3 am on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the EF-1 tornado from extreme Eastern Lincoln Parish. This EF-1 tornado, with estimated maximum winds between 95-105 mph, moved east along Jim Hinton Road, before crossing Highway 151. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted, with a large tree landing on the corner of a wooden home near the intersection of Hinton Road and Highway 151. The home suffered extensive roof damage from the falling tree. The tornado then moved northeast across Nick Spillers/Reggie Skains Road as it entered extreme Southern Union Parish. Several additional trees were snapped or uprooted before the tornado entered Union Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough of low pressure progressed east across the Southern Plains during the afternoon of April 13th, with a large warm sector of warm/humid air having expanded north across much of the Ark-La-Tex. A capping inversion delayed the onset of thunderstorm development until the late afternoon, where it eroded and large scale forcing increased with shortwave perturbations ahead of the trough and along/just ahead of a dry line as it mixed east to the Interstate 35 corridor or North and Central Texas. Given the moderate instability and strong wind shear that developed with the lead shortwave, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across Northcentral Louisiana during the late afternoon through mid evening hours, producing numerous reports of damaging winds, and several tornadoes/funnel cloud reports. While a lull in the storms was observed, a line of severe thunderstorms developed along the dryline south of the Dallas/Fort Worth Metroplex, and progressed east into East Texas by late evening, and across North Louisiana after midnight on April 14th. These severe thunderstorms exited North Louisiana just after 3 am on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The is a continuation of the Ouachita Parish EF-1 tornado. This EF-1 tornado, with estimated maximum winds between 95-105 mph, crossed Reggie Skains Road snapping several trees, and continued east across extreme Southern Union Parish just north of the Ouachita Parish line. The tornado crossed Highway 15 near the same location another EF-1 tornado that initially touched down just north of Calhoun tracked north from Ouachita Parish earlier that evening on April 13th. This tornado continued east along Highway 552 before lifting at the intersection of Highway 552 and Callaway Road. Most of the damage consisted of snapped and uprooted trees across extreme Southern Union Parish. One very large pecan tree estimated at 100 years old was lifted and dropped onto a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of Friday, April 13th, scattered supercells developed across northeast Louisiana within the broad warm sector of a strong surface cyclone centered near the Kansas/Nebraska border. These storms exhibited rotation almost immediately, and began producing tornadoes across northern Louisiana. Two tornadoes occurred in the NWS Jackson forecast area in association with a cell that tracked from near Rayville, LA north northeastward along the Boeuf River. These storms also produced strong wind as they continued into southeast Arkansas. Overnight, as the cold front associated with the storm system began to advance eastward, a quasi-linear convective system developed over the ArkLaTex and advanced eastward. These storms reached the western portion of the NWS Jackson forecast area after 2:30 am on Saturday, April 14th. Six tornadoes occurred during the early morning hours across southeast Arkansas and northeast Louisiana in association with this line of storms. The storms continued to produce damaging straight-line winds, knocking down trees and power lines in many areas, through the morning hours as it continued eastward into Mississippi. A few additional tornadoes also developed along the line, including one in Flowood, one near Noxapater, one in western Lamar County, and an EF2 tornado in Meridian.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began in open fields just west of the Bouef River and tracked northward across LA Highway 2, where it removed portions of the metal roof of an unoccupied building. The tornado continued northward along the Beouf River on the Morehouse and West Carroll Parish line just west of Benton Rd before ending shortly thereafter. Multiple trees were uprooted and trees and large tree branches were snapped along the path. Maximum estimated wind speed was 74 mph in West Carroll Parish but 95 mph for the entire tornado path. The total path length for the tornado was just under 2 miles and the entire path width was 200 yards, which occurred in Morehouse Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front worked through Louisiana during the 4th. Showers and storms occurred for a couple days ahead of the frontal passage with some storms being severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down by the Vernon Parish Sheriff's Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across much of East Texas and Southwest Arkansas by the afternoon hours on April 6th, with very warm, humid, and unstable air having spread in place across much of the Ark-La-Tex during the afternoon and evening hours. In addition to the surface based instability in place, very cold temperatures in the mid and upper level of the atmosphere resulted in very steep lapse rates further enhancing instability, with strong wind shear present in advance of a strong cold front which entered the Southern Plains during the afternoon ahead of a strong upper level trough of low pressure. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along and just in advance of the cold front, many of which became severe producing large hail and damaging winds. These storms steadily marched east across North Louisiana during the mid afternoon through much of the evening hours before diminishing late with the passage of the cold front and upper trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball size hail fell in Chatham.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado from Eastern Desoto Parish. This tornado quickly widened and intensified to EF-2 with estimated maximum winds of 125 mph shortly after crossing Bayou Pierre and entering Red River Parish, where it produced significant tree damage with numerous snapped and uprooted trees along and near Highway 177. It also snapped a few utility poles and caused significant damage to a large metal structure where it buckled the support beams as it crossed Highway 177. The tornado continued east where it paralleled Highway 371 and snapped numerous trees. The tornado was at its strongest and widest as it crossed Highway 84 near the Armistead community, where it destroyed a metal building and a few smaller metal structures along with snapping numerous utility poles. It continued to parallel Highway 84 east where it destroyed several more metal outbuildings, a farm irrigation system, and snapped more utility poles. It caused additional tree damage and snapped more utility poles along Riverfront Road before crossing the Red River and entering the south side of Coushatta. The tornado moved more east southeast where it crossed Clark Street resulting in more significant tree damage and lifting a large metal framed carport and a barn adjacent to a home. It continued moving east where it crossed Highway 480 before causing more widespread significant tree damage along Postell, Springville, and Newton Roads. The tornado then turned slightly northeast before lifting near the intersection of Bruce Street and Highway 71 where it snapped and uprooted several more trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding continued from March throughout the first half of April along the Ouachita River at Monroe, as a result of widespread heavy rainfall that fell across the Ouachita River Basin of Southcentral Arkansas and North Louisiana throughout February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding continued along the Ouachita River at Monroe through the first few days of April, before the river slowly began to recede below the 45 foot major flood stage. The water flooded the bottom of the Desiard Street (Endom) bridge, with major flooding occurring between the levees in Monroe. In addition, portions of the Forsythe Park Recreation Area (including the Forsythe Park Boat Launch) in Monroe was flooded, while water encroached on several buildings along Riverfront Street in West Monroe. A slow fall continued throughout April, with the Ouachita River at Monroe falling below the 43 foot moderate flood stage during the evening hours on April 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was stretched across the region on April 6th. As a disturbance rode along this stalled front, it brought copious amounts of rain to the region thanks to ample moisture in place. As daytime heating occurred, some storms developed which brought severe weather to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball sized hail fell in Delhi while nickel sized hail fell near Poverty Point Reservoir.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds between 90-100 mph touched down along Highway 484 near the Jerry Jones House (also called the Jones-Roque House) in the Isle Brevelle community of Melrose. Some minor roof damage with the loss of shingles occurred to a home and some of the metal roof of the Jerry Jones House was uplifted and removed. At the first home, a pickup truck parked in the grass was pushed backwards 10 feet, and a SUV was flipped onto its roof. As this tornado traveled east-northeast, most of the damage was to pecan trees that were snapped or uprooted, and had large branches broken and twisted out of the tops of the trees. The tornado crossed the Cane River snapping and uprooting more trees. A wooden electrical pole was also snapped about halfway up its length. The tornado crossed Highway 119 and moved into a pecan orchard breaking more large branches. The tornado then moved into an adjacent open field and lifted. Local residents noted that a couple of the trees that sustained significant damage along the Cane River were in excess of 200 years old.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across much of East Texas and Southwest Arkansas by the afternoon hours on April 6th, with very warm, humid, and unstable air having spread in place across much of the Ark-La-Tex during the afternoon and evening hours. In addition to the surface based instability in place, very cold temperatures in the mid and upper level of the atmosphere resulted in very steep lapse rates further enhancing instability, with strong wind shear present in advance of a strong cold front which entered the Southern Plains during the afternoon ahead of a strong upper level trough of low pressure. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along and just in advance of the cold front, many of which became severe producing large hail and damaging winds. These storms steadily marched east across North Louisiana during the mid afternoon through much of the evening hours before diminishing late with the passage of the cold front and upper trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto Pleasant Road off of Highway 371 south of Ringgold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough of low pressure progressed east across the Southern Plains during the afternoon of April 13th, with a large warm sector of warm/humid air having expanded north across much of the Ark-La-Tex. A capping inversion delayed the onset of thunderstorm development until the late afternoon, where it eroded and large scale forcing increased with shortwave perturbations ahead of the trough and along/just ahead of a dry line as it mixed east to the Interstate 35 corridor or North and Central Texas. Given the moderate instability and strong wind shear that developed with the lead shortwave, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across Northcentral Louisiana during the late afternoon through mid evening hours, producing numerous reports of damaging winds, and several tornadoes/funnel cloud reports. While a lull in the storms was observed, a line of severe thunderstorms developed along the dryline south of the Dallas/Fort Worth Metroplex, and progressed east into East Texas by late evening, and across North Louisiana after midnight on April 14th. Damaging winds and reports of hail were prevalent at first with this line of severe thunderstorms, but a number of notches developed along the squall line as these storms crossed into North Louisiana, resulting in several tornadoes touching down across areas along and north of the Interstate 20 corridor from Shreveport to Monroe. Of particular interest, the initial string of tornadoes first touched down just west of the Texas/Louisiana state line along the Harrison/Panola County line, with this supercell producing two separate tornadoes as it entered Shreveport. In fact, the tornado remained on the ground across much of western and central Shreveport, moving across the northern fringes of the property at the National Weather Service Office before moving across the Queensborough and Allendale neighborhoods and across Downtown Shreveport, before tracking across much of Bossier City along and just north of Interstate 20, crossing Interstate 220, and across the Red Chute and Princeton communities before lifting just west of the Bossier/Webster Parish line. These severe thunderstorms exited North Louisiana just after 3 am on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and branches were blown down in the Haile community. Power was out as well. Report from Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of Friday, April 13th, scattered supercells developed across northeast Louisiana within the broad warm sector of a strong surface cyclone centered near the Kansas/Nebraska border. These storms exhibited rotation almost immediately, and began producing tornadoes across northern Louisiana. Two tornadoes occurred in the NWS Jackson forecast area in association with a cell that tracked from near Rayville, LA north northeastward along the Boeuf River. These storms also produced strong wind as they continued into southeast Arkansas. Overnight, as the cold front associated with the storm system began to advance eastward, a quasi-linear convective system developed over the ArkLaTex and advanced eastward. These storms reached the western portion of the NWS Jackson forecast area after 2:30 am on Saturday, April 14th. Six tornadoes occurred during the early morning hours across southeast Arkansas and northeast Louisiana in association with this line of storms. The storms continued to produce damaging straight-line winds, knocking down trees and power lines in many areas, through the morning hours as it continued eastward into Mississippi. A few additional tornadoes also developed along the line, including one in Flowood, one near Noxapater, one in western Lamar County, and an EF2 tornado in Meridian.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were uprooted in the Beekman Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of short waves moved across the region ahead of an approaching cold front. A couple rounds of thunderstorms resulted, with one super cell producing multiple tornadoes.

A warm front was stretched across the region on April 6th. As a disturbance rode along this stalled front, it brought copious amounts of rain to the region thanks to ample moisture in place. As daytime heating occurred, some storms developed which brought severe weather to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along Calhoun Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure expanded east from the Oklahoma/Texas Panhandle region into the middle Red River Valley of Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, Northeast Texas, and North Louisiana during June 29th. Very dry conditions due to the developing drought conditions across North Louisiana combined with the compressional warming beneath the ridge resulted in afternoon temperatures climbing into the mid 90s to near 100 degrees. Limited mixing was also observed, with resultant heat indices ranging between 105 and 110 degrees.

An upper level ridge of high pressure was anchored across the Mississippi Valley west into the Central Plains during the afternoon of July 5th, with weak impulses aloft in the easterly flow interacting with strong diurnal heating to generate isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms across Northwest Louisiana and portions of East Texas. Given the strong instability that had developed that afternoon, isolated storms became severe, which resulted in damaging winds downing a tree and power lines near Shreveport and Blanchard before diminishing during the evening with the loss of heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Roy Road near Blanchard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure was anchored over the Central Plains during the afternoon of July 6th, with weak impulses aloft in the easterly flow interacting with strong diurnal heating to generate isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms across Southwest Arkansas, North Louisiana, East Texas, and Southeast Oklahoma. Given the strong instability that had developed that afternoon, some of these storms became severe, which resulted in a measured 67 mph wind gust at the Monroe Regional Airport, which downed trees near and just north of the airport in Ouachita Parish. These storms diminished during the evening with the loss of heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 67 mph wind gust was measured at the Monroe Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest flow aloft allowed for a weak shortwave trough to drop southeast into the Ark-La-Tex during the afternoon hours of July 27th, where a weak cold front pulled up stationary from just north of the DFW Metroplex to along the Arkansas/Louisiana state line. Strong heating ahead of the front allowed for afternoon temperatures to climb into the upper 90s to near 100 degrees across East Texas and North Louisiana along and just ahead of the front, resulting in moderate instability to develop across these areas. This allowed for the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms, with a couple of these storms briefly becoming severe over Northern Caddo and Bossier Parishes. Damaging winds blew down several trees and damaged a wooden fence near Blanchard and Lake Bodcau. These storms diminished during the evening with the loss of heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were snapped and a wooden fence was damaged in the Windwood Estates subdivision just off of Hilry Huckaby Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) shifted southeast across Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and extreme Northeast Texas during the morning hours of July 29th, as a shortwave trough dropped southeast from Oklahoma into Arkansas and the Lower Mississippi Valley. While this complex of showers and embedded thunderstorms entered the region as non-severe, they gradually intensified as they moved across extreme Southern Arkansas and North Louisiana, in response to an unstable atmosphere that developed due to strong daytime heating as temperatures climbed to the mid and upper 90s. As a result, scattered severe thunderstorms producing damaging winds downed trees and power lines over extreme Southern Arkansas, before they spread southward across much of North Louisiana during the mid and late afternoon hours. These storms exited the area and weakened with the loss of heating once they moved into Central and Southern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large limbs were downed in Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into a very moist and unstable airmass over southeast Louisiana. Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall with a few of the storms, causing isolated flash flooding near Baton Rouge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baton Rouge Fire Department relayed several social media reports of significant street flooding in the area. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable airmass aided the development of isolated very strong to severe thunderstorms over Terrebonne and Lafourche Parishes during the afternoon. One report of large hail was received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A delayed public report of hail up to quarter size was relayed by broadcast media. Event time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved through the region, ample moisture was present for showers and thunderstorms to develop. Many of these storms trained across portions of northeast Louisiana and over the same locations. This resulted in significant flash flooding over portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred on Highway 17 between Forest and Pioneer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a hot and humid summertime air mass, scattered thunderstorms developed by the afternoon hours of the 13th. The strongest thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds blew a power pole down onto a house in the Jones community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the overnight through the morning hours on June 3rd, along a weak cold front and associated with a trough of low pressure that drifted south across East Texas and North Louisiana along the eastern edge of an upper level ridge of high pressure over South Texas and Northeast Mexico. These storms intensified shortly after daybreak on the 3rd over portions of Northcentral Louisiana as they encountered a more unstable air mass, briefly becoming severe over Winn Parish. This resulted in damaging winds downing a large tree just southwest of Winnfield before weakening by mid-morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on Frank White Road near the intersection of Country Club Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of July 2nd, in response to a very hot and humid air mass, characteristic of temperatures near 100 degrees, and increasing low level moisture from the east from an upper low pressure system that began to drift west along the Alabama and Mississippi coasts. Given the very hot temperatures and moderate instability in place, one of these storms quickly became severe over Northern Bienville Parish, with damaging winds downing numerous trees and power lines over the communities of Gibsland and Bryceland. These storms quickly dissipated by sunset with the loss of heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed over much of Gibsland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure expanded east from the Oklahoma/Texas Panhandle region into the middle Red River Valley of Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, Northeast Texas, and North Louisiana during June 28th and 29th. Dry conditions combined with the compressional warming beneath the ridge resulted in afternoon temperatures climbing into the mid and upper 90s  each day. Limited mixing was also observed each day, with resultant heat indices ranging between 105 and 110 degrees.

Widely scattered showers and thunderstorms redeveloped over portions of Northcentral Louisiana during the late evening hours of July 31st, near a weak cold front that drifted slowly east with the passage of a positive tilt upper level trough. This trough enhanced large scale forcing near the front, with enough instability present for these storms to briefly become severe over Northern Winn Parish. Damaging winds downed a few trees near and east of the Dodson community before weakening shortly thereafter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Highway 126 just east of the Dodson community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Chandeleur Sound produced slow moving thunderstorms which produced at least 2 reports of flash flooding in the New Orleans metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of street flooding resulting in water rescues across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundaries provided a focus for slow moving thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall over portions of southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of localized street flooding were received. Observations and reports indicated that 2 to 2.5 inches of rain fell in under 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across Northern Desoto, much of Lincoln, and Western Union Parishes to start the month of September. However, scattered showers and thunderstorms increased in coverage between September 7th-11th across these areas, ahead of a weak surface frontal system that pulled up stationary across portions of East Texas and North Louisiana. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1.50-2.00 inches fell across the aforementioned severe drought areas of North Louisiana, resulting in a one category improvement to moderate (D1) drought by the September 13th issuance of the U.S. Drought Monitor. Additional heavy rainfall of 3.00-4.00+ inches that fell during the final two weeks of September eliminated drought conditions across much of North Louisiana, as short and long term moisture deficits had completely recovered.

Extreme (D3) drought continued across Northern Bossier, much of Webster, Northern Bienville, and Southwest Claiborne Parishes to start the month of September, while severe (D2) drought conditions encompassed the remainder of these parishes. However, scattered showers and thunderstorms increased in coverage between September 3rd-11th across these areas, ahead of a weak surface frontal system that pulled up stationary across portions of East Texas and North Louisiana. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1.50-3.00+ inches fell across much of these areas, resulting in slow improvements in drought across portions of this area by the September 13th issuance of the U.S. Drought Monitor. Additional heavy rainfall of 3.00-6.00 inches that fell during the final two weeks of September eliminated drought conditions across much of North Louisiana, as short and long term moisture deficits had completely recovered.

Very dry conditions due to the ongoing drought conditions across much of the region combined with the compressional warming beneath the ridge resulted in afternoon temperatures climbing into the mid 90s areawide. Limited mixing was also observed, with resultant heat indices ranging between 105 and 110 degrees.

An upper level ridge of high pressure was anchored over the Central Plains during the afternoon of July 6th, with weak impulses aloft in the easterly flow interacting with strong diurnal heating to generate isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms across Southwest Arkansas, North Louisiana, East Texas, and Southeast Oklahoma. Given the strong instability that had developed that afternoon, some of these storms became severe, which resulted in a measured 67 mph wind gust at the Monroe Regional Airport, which downed trees near and just north of the airport in Ouachita Parish. These storms diminished during the evening with the loss of heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of the roof of the Brookshire's warehouse on Kansas Lane was peeled back due to a microburst near the Monroe Regional Airport.

Severe drought conditions continued throughout July across much of Caddo, Bossier, and Webster Parishes, in response to a prolonged period of above normal temperatures and much below rainfall since May. July recorded above normal temperatures of 3-4+ degrees across these areas, where monthly rainfall totals only ranged between one and two inches. In fact, Shreveport ranked as the 8th driest May-July on record with 4.43 inches measured, and also the 3rd warmest May-July on record at 82.9 degrees, which was 0.6 degrees warmer than the exceptional drought year of 2011, and just 0.6 degrees cooler than the significantly warm and dry year of 1998. Much of Northwest Louisiana reached or exceeded 100 degrees on multiple days between July 16th-26th, with temperatures exceeding 105 degrees on the 21st and 22nd. In fact, Shreveport recorded its hottest day in July with 108 degrees recorded on July 22nd. Multiple wildfires were reported across the area throughout July, with burn bans in effect for much of the month. Given these extreme temperatures and continued lack of significant rains, extreme drought (D3) conditions were introduced across these areas in the July 24th issuance of the U.S Drought Monitor.

A large mid and upper level ridge of high pressure expanded from the Southern Rockies across all of the Ark-La-Tex during July 19th-22nd. Very dry conditions due to the ongoing drought conditions across much of North Louisiana combined with the compressional warming beneath the ridge resulted in afternoon temperatures ranging from 100-108 degrees each day. Limited mixing was also observed, with resultant and oppressive heat indices ranging between 106 to 112 degrees on the 19th-21st. A cold front slowly shifted southwest across North Louisiana on July 22nd, with additional compressional warming along and just south of the front resulting in the hottest temperatures for the month of July on record in Shreveport, with oppressively high heat indices ranging from 110 to 116 degrees. Slightly cooler and drier air spilled southwest across North Louisiana in wake of the frontal passage on the 23rd.

Limited mixing was also observed, with resultant and oppressive heat indices ranging between 106 to 112 degrees on the 19th-21st. A cold front slowly shifted southwest across North Louisiana on July 22nd, with additional compressional warming along and just south of the front resulting in the hottest temperatures for the month of July on record in Shreveport, with oppressively high heat indices ranging from 110 to 116 degrees. Slightly cooler and drier air spilled southwest across North Louisiana in wake of the frontal passage on the 23rd.

A large mid and upper level ridge of high pressure expanded from the Southern Rockies across all of the Ark-La-Tex during July 19th-23rd. Very dry conditions due to the ongoing drought conditions across much of North Louisiana combined with the compressional warming beneath the ridge resulted in afternoon temperatures ranging from 100-108 degrees each day. Limited mixing was also observed, with resultant and oppressive heat indices ranging between 106 to 112 degrees on the 19th-21st. A cold front slowly shifted southwest across North Louisiana on July 22nd, with additional compressional warming along and just south of the front resulting in near record heat for the month of July with temperatures near or in excess of 105 degrees, and oppressively high heat indices ranging from 107 to 114 degrees. Slightly cooler and drier air spilled southwest across Westcentral Louisiana in wake of the frontal passage late in the day on the 23rd.

Remnants of a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) shifted southeast across Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and extreme Northeast Texas during the morning hours of July 29th, as a shortwave trough dropped southeast from Oklahoma into Arkansas and the Lower Mississippi Valley. While this complex of showers and embedded thunderstorms entered the region as non-severe, they gradually intensified as they moved across extreme Southern Arkansas and North Louisiana, in response to an unstable atmosphere that developed due to strong daytime heating as temperatures climbed to the mid and upper 90s. As a result, scattered severe thunderstorms producing damaging winds downed trees and power lines over extreme Southern Arkansas, before they spread southward across much of North Louisiana during the mid and late afternoon hours. These storms exited the area and weakened with the loss of heating once they moved into Central and Southern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large limbs were downed along Greenway Place in Shreveport. A tree also fell onto a parked truck as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered showers and thunderstorms redeveloped over portions of Northcentral Louisiana during the late evening hours of July 31st, near a weak cold front that drifted slowly east with the passage of a positive tilt upper level trough. This trough enhanced large scale forcing near the front, with enough instability present for these storms to briefly become severe over Northern Winn Parish. Damaging winds downed a few trees near and east of the Dodson community before weakening shortly thereafter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the community of Eros.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across Northern Desoto, much of Lincoln, and Western Union Parishes to start the month of September. However, scattered showers and thunderstorms increased in coverage between September 7th-11th across these areas, ahead of a weak surface frontal system that pulled up stationary across portions of East Texas and North Louisiana. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1.50-2.00 inches fell across the aforementioned severe drought areas of North Louisiana, resulting in a one category improvement to moderate (D1) drought by the September 13th issuance of the U.S. Drought Monitor. Additional heavy rainfall of 3.00-4.00+ inches that fell during the final two weeks of September eliminated drought conditions across much of North Louisiana, as short and long term moisture deficits had completely recovered.

Severe drought conditions developed during the first week of July across much of Desoto, Red River, Bienville, and Claiborne Parishes in Northwest Louisiana, in response to a prolonged period of above normal temperatures and much below rainfall since May. July recorded above normal temperatures of 3-4 degrees across these areas, where monthly rainfall totals only ranged between one and two inches, with isolated higher amounts. Much of Northwest Louisiana reached or exceeded 100 degrees on multiple days between July 16th-26th, with temperatures exceeding 105 degrees on the 21st and 22nd.  As a result of the (near) record heat and dryness, burn bans were in effect for much of the month for these areas. Eventually, extreme drought (D3) conditions were introduced across much of Bienville and Claiborne Parishes in the July 24th issuance of the U.S Drought Monitor.

Severe drought conditions developed during the third week of July across much of Union Parish in Northcentral Louisiana, in response to a prolonged period of above normal temperatures and much below rainfall since May. July recorded above normal temperatures of 3-4+ degrees across much of this area, where monthly rainfall totals only ranged between one and two inches. Much of Northcentral Louisiana climbed into the upper 90s to in excess of 100 degrees on multiple days between July 16th-27th. As a result of this heat wave and dryness, a burn ban went into effect for Union Parish for much of the month.

This resulted in much above normal temperatures areawide, with readings climbing into the lower 90s in the afternoon. ||A 6 month old girl died from heat stroke on May 15th after being left inside a hot car for about 6 hours, from around 11 am to 5 pm in the 6700 block of Woolworth Road in Shreveport. The high temperature in Shreveport on May 15th was 91 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot temperatures settled in across Northwest Louisiana during May 15th, with the high temperature in Shreveport climbing to 91 degrees. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture combined with an upper level disturbance produced scattered showers and storms over South Central Louisiana. One storm became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported hail of one inch in diameter in Church Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with ample low level moisture to produce scattered to numerous thunderstorms across the region. One storm became severe in Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded on a spotter's home weather station north of Jennings. A few tree limbs were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the day on May 25th across Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana, along an outflow boundary that had developed earlier in the morning from a complex of showers and thunderstorms over Southeast Oklahoma. Daytime heating and ample low level moisture contributed to a moderately unstable air mass during the afternoon, with some of these storms briefly becoming severe across portions of Northeast Texas and Northwest Louisiana. Damaging winds from localized microbursts occurred at Barksdale Air Force Base as well as the Cullen and Springhill communities in Northwest Louisiana, where trees were blown down and large limbs were snapped.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst produced a peak wind gust of 62 mph at Barksdale Air Force Base in Bossier City. Large tree limbs, some over 5 inches in diameter, were snapped across portions of the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shear axis lingered from the Southeast Texas coast north across East Texas into Eastern Oklahoma and Western Arkansas on May 23rd. Strong heating and ample low level moisture near this shear axis allowed for moderate instability by afternoon, contributing to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and North Louisiana. An isolated severe thunderstorm developed near Blanchard, Louisiana, where quarter size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell on the north side of Blanchard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure over the central Gulf Coast combined with increasingly moist and unstable air to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.
An upper level trough of low pressure over the central Gulf Coast combined with increasingly moist and unstable air to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported near Harrison Avenue and US Highway 190.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical like atmosphere was observed across all of the Four State Region and this moisture was being fed into a trough of low pressure centered across North Central Texas. Lift in association with the upper level trough and a stalled frontal boundary near the Interstate 20 Corridor of Northeast Texas and Northern Louisiana resulted in |widespread heavy rainfall across portions of the region. One particular storm moved into the Shreveport and Bossier City area during the afternoon hours of Sept. 22nd and produced rainfall rates in excess of 3 inches per hour over Shreveport. This produced high water and road closures across numerous thoroughfares in both cities. A water rescue was even reported in Shreveport. The rainfall set a rainfall record for the day as well, where 3.83 inches was recorded at the Shreveport Regional Airport, surpassing the previous record of 2.47 inches set in 1965. Additional flooding occurred during the early evening hours across Shreveport as additional storms moved from south to north into the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of North Hearne Avenue and North Market Street was flooded.

Additional flooding occurred during the early evening hours across Shreveport as additional storms moved from south to north into the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of North Market Street and Freestate Blvd was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought continued across Southern Caddo Parish including much of Shreveport to start the month of September, while severe (D2) drought conditions encompassed all but the northern portions of the parish. However, scattered showers and thunderstorms increased in coverage between September 3rd-11th across these areas, ahead of a weak surface frontal system that pulled up stationary across portions of East Texas and North Louisiana. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1.50-2.00 inches fell across much of Caddo Parish, resulting in slow improvements in drought across portions of this area by the September 13th issuance of the U.S. Drought Monitor. Additional heavy rainfall of 3.00-6.00 inches that fell during the final two weeks of September eliminated drought conditions across much of North Louisiana, as short and long term moisture deficits had completely recovered. Flashing flooding was also observed across portions of Shreveport as a result of excessive heavy rainfall on September 22nd. The consistent rains throughout the month resulted in Shreveport recording its 6th wettest September on record with 9.10 inches recorded at the Shreveport Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Collisions between thunderstorm outflow boundaries and lake breeze boundaries produced nearly stationary thunderstorms south of Lake Pontchartrain. Several reports of flooding were received from the New Orleans Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media posts from local broadcast media outlets showed water entering vehicles in the New Orleans Central Business District.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Collisions between thunderstorm outflow boundaries and lake breeze boundaries produced nearly stationary thunderstorms south of Lake Pontchartrain. Several reports of flooding were received from the New Orleans Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was reported flooded in St. Rose. Numerous other roads in the parish reported street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak outflow boundary leftover from showers and thunderstorms that affected portions of extreme Southern Arkansas, East Texas, and Northwest Louisiana during the afternoon and evening of August 8th, lingered near the Interstate 20 corridor of Northwest Louisiana during the morning hours on August 9th. Meanwhile, a closed upper low over Eastern Kansas dove south into Northeast Oklahoma before turning more east into Northern Arkansas during the afternoon hours of the 9th. Showers and thunderstorms which developed during the morning over Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas reinforced the outflow boundary over extreme Northwest Louisiana, with large scale forcing ahead of the closed low increasing during the afternoon to trigger scattered showers and thunderstorms. One of these storms became strong over Bossier City, with gusty winds peeling a metal roof off of a storage building before weakening during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal roof was peeled off of a storage building in Bossier City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed over North Texas during August 11th-12th, which helped to enhance the seabreeze as it spread north northeast into East Texas and Western Louisiana during the afternoon hours on the 12th. These storms intensified during the afternoon as they surged northeast, producing a swath of damaging winds which downed numerous trees across East Texas. These storms weakened as they moved into extreme Northwest Louisiana during the late afternoon and early evening hours, but not before downing a tree in South Shreveport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near the intersection of Williamson Way and Mansfield Road in South Shreveport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual August cool front shifted south across East Texas, Southern Arkansas, and North Louisiana during the afternoon hours of August 20th, south of an upper low and attendant upper trough that tracked east across the Mid and Lower Mississippi Valleys. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon through the evening hours along the front over Northcentral Louisiana, where greater large scale forcing ahead of the trough interacted with strong instability as temperatures had climbed into the upper 90s to near 100 degrees ahead of the front. Some of these storms became severe, producing multiple reports of wind damage which downed numerous trees and power lines. A 76 mph wind gust was measured at the Monroe Regional Airport in Ouachita Parish, with an estimated 60 mph wind gust also observed in Dubach in Lincoln Parish. Unfortunately, a tree also fell onto a mobile home near the Spearsville community in Union Parish, killing a woman inside of the home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were downed along Highway 545 southeast of the Lisbon community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon seabreeze spread north into Northcentral Louisiana from the Louisiana coast during the afternoon hours of August 6th, and interacted with a weak upper trough which lingered over the Lower Mississippi Valley. Temperatures climbed into the upper 90s across Northcentral Louisiana during the afternoon, generating moderate instability which allowed these scattered showers and thunderstorms to intensify over Western Winn Parish. As a result, these storms briefly became severe, which produced damaging winds west-southwest of the Calvin community, before diminishing during the evening with the loss of heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed on Lane Road just off of Highway 156.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) and severe (D2) drought conditions continued throughout the entire month of August across much of Caddo, Desoto, Bossier, Webster, Bienville, Claiborne, and Western Union Parishes in North Louisiana, as continued very hot and dry conditions persisted over these areas. In fact, the worse of the extreme drought was centered over much of Caddo, Bossier, Webster, Southwest Claiborne, and Northern Bienville Parishes, although beneficial rains that fell late in the month did help to ease drought conditions slightly over Northern Caddo and Western Bossier Parishes. August monthly rainfall totals ranged from three-quarter of an inch up to two inches across much of these drought stricken areas, which was some one to two inches below normal. With temperatures climbing into the mid 90s to near 100 degrees throughout much of the month, drought conditions continued to worsen before slowly improving across portions of the area to end the month.
Afternoon thunderstorms in South Louisiana produced a landspout near Lake Arthur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures posted to social media indicated a landspout occurred near the town of Lake Arthur. No damage was reported as the landspout remained in a pasture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm near Bundick Lake produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tin roofing material was blown off a carport and outbuilding near Bundick Lake. Tree branches and power lines were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across much of Red River Parish to start the month of August, with hot and dry conditions persisting across this area through the second week of the month. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across portions of Northwest Louisiana (including Red River Parish) during August 10-12th, ahead of an upper level low pressure system over portions of the Southern Plains, and north of the typical summer Gulf of Mexico upper ridge. Rainfall amounts ranged from an inch to an inch and a half across much of Red River Parish, which resulted in a one category improvement to moderate (D1) drought across this area in the August 16th issuance of the U.S. Drought Monitor.

Severe (D2) drought conditions developed across much of Lincoln Parish to start the second week of August, as a result of prolonged above normal temperatures and dryness which developed throughout July and early August. Monthly rainfall amounts during August ranged from three-quarters of an inch to around two inches across Lincoln Parish, which was some one to two inches below normal. Temperatures in the mid 90s to near 100 degrees during the middle and latter half of the month only helped to exasperate drought conditions,  which continued into September.

Much above normal temperatures and much below normal rainfall contributed to severe drought conditions developing across portions of Northwest Louisiana including much of Caddo, Bossier, and Webster Parishes. Only 1.76 inches of rain fell from early May through all of June in Shreveport (Caddo Parish), which was 19% of normal during what is typically the wettest period of the year. Only 2.39 inches of rain also fell from early May throughout all of June in Minden (Webster Parish), which was 25% of normal. This extensive dryness, combined with temperatures that ranged from 4-6 degrees above normal during May, and 3-4 degrees above normal during June, resulted in many farmers/producers losing many of their non-irrigated crops, as well as increased instances of wildfires and heightened fire danger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough developed over the Four Corners Region on December 26th, before taking on a negative tilt as it ejected northeast across Northwest and North Texas and much of Oklahoma during the early morning hours of the 27th. Strong southerly winds ahead of this upper trough resulted in warm, humid conditions spreading north across East Texas, North Louisiana, and extreme Southern Arkansas, resulting in weak to moderate surface based instability over these areas. Wind shear was also very strong ahead of this system, with the rapidly increasing moisture return and subtle forcing ahead of the trough allowing for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and especially during the evening over East Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, and adjacent sections of Southwest Arkansas. A  squall line developed over Northern and Central Texas along the ejecting upper trough, with these thunderstorms racing east across East Texas and Southeast Oklahoma during the late evening hours on the 26th, and into North Louisiana during the early morning hours of the 27th. Damaging winds downed trees across Caddo, Bossier, Desoto, and Southern Natchitoches Parishes, before these storms weakened prior to daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across North Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across the Four State Region during the predawn hours of December 14th in response to a vigorous, closed upper level low pressure system across Central and Southeast Texas. At the surface, an occluded frontal boundary was located across Northern Louisiana and some surface based instability was |located along this boundary. Very cold temperatures aloft contributed to steep lapse rates throughout the atmosphere and the end result were several hail producing thunderstorms.  Hail ranged from as small as pea size to as large as half dollars across portions of Northwest Louisiana during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell along Highway 371 between Sibley and Minden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall across the Wallace Lake basin of Northwest Louisiana on December 26th-27th resulted in moderate flooding developing on Wallace Lake along the Caddo/Desoto Parish line on the final day of 2018, which continued through the first week of 2019 before slowly receding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall of two to four inches fell across the Wallace Lake basin along the Southern Caddo/Northern Desoto Parish line on December 26th-27th, atop already saturated grounds where rainfall amounts ranged from ten to in excess of twelve inches during December. As a result, Wallace Lake reached its 151 foot flood stage at 930 am on December 31st, and continued to steadily rise to near 153 feet during the first week of January 2019. ||The flooding resulted in the closure of Linwood Avenue from Tyre Road to the Desoto Parish line (including Linwood Avenue at the Southern Loop and Barron Road at Linwood Avenue). Homes across these areas were cut off due to the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall across the Wallace Lake basin of Northwest Louisiana on December 26th-27th resulted in moderate flooding developing on Wallace Lake along the Caddo/Desoto Parish line on the final day of 2018, which continued through the first week of 2019 before slowly receding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall of two to four inches fell across the Wallace Lake basin along the Southern Caddo/Northern Desoto Parish line on December 26th-27th, atop already saturated grounds where rainfall amounts ranged from ten to in excess of twelve inches during December. As a result, Wallace Lake reached its 151 foot flood stage at 930 am on December 31st, and continued to steadily rise to near 153 feet during the first week of January 2019. The flooding resulted in the closure of Linwood Avenue at the Desoto/Caddo Parish line, as well as Keithville-Kingston Road at Missile Base Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough amplified during the daytime hours of October 31st across much of the Plains and Southern Rockies, which reinforced a strong cold front southeast into the Southern Plains. Ahead of this front and accompanying trough, above normal temperatures ranging from the mid 70s to lower 80s coupled with increased low level moisture resulted in a moderately unstable air mass during the afternoon. The pressure gradient tightened across the warm sector which expanded across much of the Ark-La-Tex, well ahead of the front as surface low pressure intensified along the frontal surface over North Texas. This enhanced wind shear as the front advanced east across much of Texas and Southeast Oklahoma, with large scale forcing ahead of the trough contributing to scattered shower and thunderstorm development during the afternoon over Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and Central Texas. Some of these storms became severe of portions of Southwest Arkansas and extreme Northeast Texas, with a squall line developing farther west over Central Texas by mid and late afternoon, which quickly advanced east across much of East Texas and North Louisiana during the late afternoon and evening hours. Several reports of wind damage which downed trees and power lines were received across East Texas and Northwest Louisiana. This squall line weakened as it progressed east across Northcentral Louisiana, but a discrete supercell thunderstorm developed out ahead of the main line over Southwest Rapides Parish Louisiana, and spawned a nearly 40 mile long track tornado that remained on the ground from just southwest of Alexandria and across Southeast Grant and Central La Salle Parishes just prior to and shortly after midnight on November 1st. These storms quickly exited the southeast sections of Northcentral Louisiana by 2 am on the 1st, but not before spawning additional tornadoes and widespread wind damage across much of Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the continuation of the Southeast Grant Parish EF-2 tornado. This tornado, with estimated maximum winds of 134 mph, continued northeast across the heavily wooded areas just southeast of Highway 8, until it crossed Cecil Road where a double wide mobile home on cinder blocks was lifted, flipped, and moved approximately 100 yards. Fortunately, the couple living in the mobile home received the Tornado Warning via Wireless Emergency Alerts and moved to a nearby home before the mobile home was destroyed. The tornado tracked northeast across Highway 774 where numerous trees were snapped or uprooted, before moving over Mayo Road. It was here where a mobile home was completely destroyed with five other mobile homes, three structures, and four family homes suffering partial damage. The tornado crossed Highway 8 and along Highway 772, where the roof of a mobile home was partially ripped off, and a resident was trapped in a nearby home by falling trees. The tornado then moved through the community of Trout, where additional trees were snapped or uprooted and power lines downed along Highway 84 and Church Street. It continued northeast across Biltmore Drive, and across a heavily wooded area before finally lifting near an unnamed dirt road just off of Highway 127 a couple of miles northwest of Jena. The total path length of this tornado from when it initially touched down just southwest of Alexandria (in Rapides Parish), to when it lifted northwest of Jena (in La Salle Parish) was approximately 38 miles.||The La Salle Parish Office of Homeland Security and Emergency Preparedness estimates that 82 homes were damaged, eleven mobile homes were destroyed, as well as damage to two business and one public building. The Trout Post Office received minor damage as well as Ganey Equipment and a Methodist Church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Michael was first classified as a Potential Tropical Cyclone on October 6th in the northwest Caribbean Sea. It became a Tropical Storm at midday on the 7th, and a Hurricane on the morning of the 8th as it passed by the western tip of Cuba. Michael continued to strengthen to a Category 4 hurricane before making landfall near Panama City, Florida during the afternoon of the 9th. The pressure gradient between the tropical system and high pressure over the eastern U.S. caused a long duration of moderate east to southeast winds across the northern Gulf of Mexico resulting in higher than normal tides across southeast Louisiana and coastal Mississippi. ||Impacts over the local area were limited to marine wind and minor coastal flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Ocean Service gauge at Pilots Station East reported 2.5 feet of storm surge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the south and spawned an outbreak of tornadoes in Southeast Texas and Louisiana. The severe weather began late in the evening and went into early November 1st. A few tornadoes were long lived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began near Shelby Lane north of HWY 108. The tornado continued east northeast for several miles crossing Choupique road, Thompson road, and Johnny Jones roads before ending around HWY 27. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed in Rapides Parish. The most significant damage was to a new home on Bordelon Road, built of steel beams and metal sheets. A large roll up door was blown in, and picked up an outside AC unit, tossing it into the garage. The steel beams were bent in three-fourths of the building. Half of the metal roof was blown off, and what remained shifted several inches. One exterior wall shifted several inches. Several other homes on Bordelon Street had part of their roofs removed. Many trees were snapped and power lines were downed. The tornado continued on past midnight for several minutes and into November 1st. The peak estimated wind speed was 122 MPH.

The tornado dissipated near LA 391 northwest of Dry Creek. Estimated peak winds were 110 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the ArkLaMiss region from west to east during the overnight hours of October 31st and November 1st. This line brought damaging winds and tornadoes to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree and a few branches were blown down in Bonita.

The tornado continued northeast moving through the Martin Parks Subdivision where is primarily snapped tree limbs and then dissipated between MacArthur Drive and Interstate 49. Max sustained winds were estimated at 99 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary provided the focus for nearly stationary thunderstorms. One of these storms produced street flooding in downtown New Orleans during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of street flooding were received from the Middle Garden District in Downtown New Orleans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the south and spawned an outbreak of tornadoes in Southeast Texas and Louisiana. The severe weather began late in the evening and went into early November 1st. A few tornadoes were long lived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado started near Hwy 165 south of Alexandria. The storm removed the roof of a restaurant on MacArthur Drive, and several other businesses had roof damage as well. Behind the mall, a bowling alley had a wall collapse. Significant roof damage occurred to several businesses in the Lee Street/Overton Street region. The tornado crossed the Red River, and moved from the south side of Highway 167 to the north side of Highway 167 in Pineville. Numerous trees and power lines were downed on the campus of Louisiana College and surrounding areas. Some of these trees did land on homes and vehicles. As the tornado continued north-northeast, it snapped more trees and power lines in the Kingsville region, including Donahue Ferry Road, Pearce Road, Pinecrest, and Camp Beauregard. East of the Ball-Paradise region, many large trees were downed along Washboard Road, damaging some homes and buildings. This tornado continued into Grant Parish. Maximum estimated winds were 135 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the south and spawned an outbreak of tornadoes in Southeast Texas and Louisiana. The severe weather began late in the evening and went into early November 1st. A few tornadoes were long lived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado damaged 1 home and broke small branches along Granger Lane in Cameron Parish. The tornado developed in a field then dissipated in a field before reaching the next street. Maximum estimated winds were 79 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow storm system moved across the region causing high tides and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High tide produced minor flooding around Cypremort Point. The tide level reached 4.3 feet at high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough amplified during the daytime hours of October 31st across much of the Plains and Southern Rockies, which reinforced a strong cold front southeast into the Southern Plains. Ahead of this front and accompanying trough, above normal temperatures ranging from the mid 70s to lower 80s coupled with increased low level moisture resulted in a moderately unstable air mass during the afternoon. The pressure gradient tightened across the warm sector which expanded across much of the Ark-La-Tex, well ahead of the front as surface low pressure intensified along the frontal surface over North Texas. This enhanced wind shear as the front advanced east across much of Texas and Southeast Oklahoma, with large scale forcing ahead of the trough contributing to scattered shower and thunderstorm development during the afternoon over Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, and Central Texas. Some of these storms became severe of portions of Southwest Arkansas and extreme Northeast Texas, with a squall line developing farther west over Central Texas by mid and late afternoon, which quickly advanced east across much of East Texas and North Louisiana during the late afternoon and evening hours. Several reports of wind damage which downed trees and power lines were received across East Texas and Northwest Louisiana. This squall line weakened as it progressed east across Northcentral Louisiana, but a discrete supercell thunderstorm developed out ahead of the main line over Southwest Rapides Parish Louisiana, and spawned a nearly 40 mile long track tornado that remained on the ground from just southwest of Alexandria and across Southeast Grant and Central La Salle Parishes just prior to and shortly after midnight on November 1st. These storms quickly exited the southeast sections of Northcentral Louisiana by 2 am on the 1st, but not before spawning additional tornadoes and widespread wind damage across much of Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed across Gorman Road just south of Blanchard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado with maximum estimated winds near 134 mph continued on the ground from Northern Rapides Parish into Southeast Grant Parish, where numerous trees were snapped or uprooted along Clear Creek Road and Barney Durand Road. A vehicle trailer was picked up and pushed around a downed tree on Barney Durand Road as well. The tornado tracked northeast to Dug Leckie Road, where a mobile home with two adults and five children was destroyed. Two of these family members suffered minor injuries. The mobile home itself was anchored into the ground with two foot anchors, but no concrete reinforcement. It was lifted out of the ground and flipped, moving approximately 20 yards. The iron frame of the mobile home was ripped off and thrown a couple hundred yards. Additional homes were damaged and trees snapped or uprooted on Hardwater Lake Road near Spur 89 Road, before the tornado moved into Southwest La Salle Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Michael was first classified as a Potential Tropical Cyclone on October 6th in the northwest Caribbean Sea. It became a Tropical Storm at midday on the 7th, and a Hurricane on the morning of the 8th as it passed by the western tip of Cuba. Michael continued to strengthen to a Category 4 hurricane before making landfall near Panama City, Florida during the afternoon of the 9th. The pressure gradient between the tropical system and high pressure over the eastern U.S. caused a long duration of moderate east to southeast winds across the northern Gulf of Mexico resulting in higher than normal tides across southeast Louisiana and coastal Mississippi. ||Impacts over the local area were limited to marine wind and minor coastal flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 2 to 2.5 feet of surge on Lake Pontchartrain was noted at USGS and COE gauges at the Rigolets and at Mandeville during the high tide cycle overnight on the 9th-10th. This resulted in minor to moderate flooding of low lying roadways near Lake Pontchartrain, with a few roads becoming impassible. The highest water levels were observed in the Palm Lake area and other low lying portions of Slidell. Water also covered Lakeshore Drive in Mandeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving through the Lower Mississippi River Valley into the Ohio River Valley pushed a cold front through southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi on the 1st. Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of the front, producing numerous reports of severe weather, including several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado touched down along Highway 60, moved north-northeast across Highway 439 and continued to just north of Mack Adams Oberry Road. Where the tornado touched down along Highway 60, numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. A single family home had a large portion of its roof removed and one exterior wall blown in. Several trailers were heavily damaged including one rolled trailer, injuring its two occupants. Estimated peak wind was 115 mph, path length 2.25 miles, path width 200 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down along Louisiana Highway 1049 just west of Interstate 55, where minor tree damage occurred with one tree snapped. The tornado proceeded east with mainly just some light tree damage as it crossed I-55. The strongest damage occurred along Wren Street, where multiple trees were snapped and uprooted, along with numerous tree limbs damaged. At the end of Wren St., two large pine trees were uprooted. One fell through and destroyed a mobile home. Other minor structural damage occurred to a few residences where mainly shingles were ripped off. The tornado continued eastward before finally lifting along Louisiana Highway 1051. The estimated peak wind was 90 mph, path length 0.6 miles, path width 50 yards.

A large number of trees were snapped or uprooted. An outbuilding was destroyed, and several camper trailers rolled. A single family home had part of the roof removed with damage to the porch. Estimated peak wind was 115 mph, path length 0.66 miles, path width 200 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down just north of Medical Center Drive and snapped multiple trees west of Veterans Avenue. The tornado crossed Veterans Avenue and led to further tree damage along with some minor to moderate structural damage. Minor damage occurred to a hotel, and also to a small office building. A few windows were blown out at the hotel. The tornado continued eastward, where it then caused moderate roof damage to a small professional building. Some roof decking was ripped off and multiple dormers were ripped off or damaged. The tornado continued eastward and quickly dissipated as it moved back into the woods. Estimated peak wind was 100 mph, path length 0.51 miles, path width 75 yards.
